title: Reading and Use of English - Part 7
description: You are going to read an article about four people who describe their experiences with sports. For questions 43–52, choose from the people (A–D). The people may be chosen more than once.

content.title: Playing the Game: Four People on Sports and Competition

texts.A:
### Chloe, 24, Physiotherapy Student
[43]I've been playing competitive tennis since I was nine and reached a national junior ranking before injury forced me to take a long break.[/43] Coming back was harder mentally than physically – I had lost all my confidence and had to rebuild my game from scratch. [47]That experience made me choose physiotherapy as a career because I wanted to help other athletes go through the same recovery process.[/47] [51]What I love about tennis above all is the mental battle – it's as much a psychological sport as a physical one, and managing your emotions under pressure is the hardest skill to master.[/51]
texts.B:
### Pete, 50, Plumber
I've played in a Sunday morning football league for twenty years. [44]At my level, the results barely matter – what keeps us all coming back is the friendship and the routine of it.[/44] We go for breakfast together after every match. A few of the lads have been playing together since school. [48]Over the years I've had two operations on my knee but I refuse to stop playing because this team is genuinely part of my identity.[/48] [52]I coach my daughter's under-twelve team on Saturday mornings as well, which has given me a completely new perspective on the game.[/52] Watching children discover their ability for the first time is something special.
texts.C:
### Tania, 38, Marketing Executive
[45]I completed my first marathon last spring after eighteen months of training and it remains the proudest moment of my sporting life.[/45] I'd never thought of myself as an athlete before. [49]The training schedule was brutal and there were many Sunday mornings when I seriously considered giving up, but I kept going because I had told so many people I was doing it.[/49] I think public commitment is enormously powerful. [53]I cried when I crossed the finish line, not just from exhaustion but from genuine disbelief that I had done it.[/53] I'm already signed up for another one next year.
texts.D:
### Yusuf, 16, School Pupil
[46]I joined the school swimming team at the start of secondary school mostly because my parents wanted me to do a sport, not because I had any particular interest.[/46] But three years on, I'm training six mornings a week and have set a county record for my age group. [50]The thing about competitive swimming that surprises most people is how mentally exhausting it is – the discipline required to train alone in a pool before school every day takes a very specific kind of focus.[/50] [54]My coach has told me I have the potential to compete at a higher level, and I'm trying not to let that pressure affect my enjoyment of the sport.[/54]

questions.question: had a sporting career interrupted by a physical setback?
questions.explanation: Chloe's competitive tennis career was interrupted by an injury that forced her to take a long break.

questions.question: values the social bonds of their sport more than winning or losing?
questions.explanation: Pete says at his level the results barely matter and what keeps everyone coming back is the friendship and routine.

questions.question: achieved a significant sporting goal after a long period of preparation?
questions.explanation: Tania completed her first marathon last spring after eighteen months of training, calling it her proudest sporting moment.

questions.question: initially had no real desire to take up the sport they now pursue seriously?
questions.explanation: Yusuf joined the swimming team mainly because his parents wanted him to do a sport, not out of personal interest.

questions.question: was influenced to choose their career path by a personal experience in sport?
questions.explanation: Chloe chose physiotherapy as a career because she wanted to help other athletes through the same recovery she experienced.

questions.question: has suffered physical problems but refuses to give up participating?
questions.explanation: Pete has had two knee operations but refuses to stop playing because the team is part of his identity.

questions.question: was motivated to continue with a challenge partly by not wanting to let others down?
questions.explanation: Tania kept going because she had told so many people she was doing the marathon, making public commitment a powerful motivator.

questions.question: highlights a hidden difficulty of their sport that people outside it tend not to realise?
questions.explanation: Yusuf says the mental exhaustion and discipline required for competitive swimming surprises most people.

questions.question: believes the most demanding aspect of their sport is psychological rather than physical?
questions.explanation: Chloe says managing emotions under pressure is the hardest skill in tennis, making it as much a psychological sport as a physical one.

questions.question: has gained a fresh understanding of sport through involvement with younger players?
questions.explanation: Pete coaches his daughter's under-twelve team and says it has given him a completely new perspective on the game.
